artifacts.summary:
## Overview
The meeting discussed the financial sustainability of the higher education sector in Wales, including the impact of demographic changes, pension costs, and Brexit uncertainty. The Higher Education Funding Council for Wales (HEFCW) provided an update on their funding allocations and priorities for the 2019-20 academic year. The sector is facing significant financial challenges, but HEFCW is working to support and fund growth in part-time provision and improve the accessibility of information on university income and expenditure.

## Key Discussion Points
### Financial Sustainability
* The sector is facing significant financial challenges due to demographic changes, pension costs, and Brexit uncertainty.
* Universities are managing these challenges, but may need to take out more costs, leading to job losses and reduced capacity.
* HEFCW is working to support and fund growth in part-time provision.
* The sector is also facing challenges in maintaining its attractiveness to students and research capacity.

### Governance and Risk
* HEFCW is monitoring the financial health of each university and has identified one university as high risk, five as medium, and two as low in the short to medium term.
* The sector is working to improve governance arrangements and risk management, including an independent review of governance in Wales.
* HEFCW is also working to improve the relationships between executive teams and governors.

### Funding and Allocations
* HEFCW has allocated £149 million for the 2019-20 academic year, including £7 million for recurrent teaching funding and £76 million for research funding.
* The sector is also receiving £15 million in innovation funding to help universities access UKRI funding.
* HEFCW is working to improve the accessibility of information on university income and expenditure.

### Research and Innovation
* The sector is facing challenges in maintaining research funding, particularly in the wake of Brexit.
* HEFCW is working to support the sector in accessing UKRI funding and is consulting on a £15 million innovation funding package.
* The sector is also working to improve its research capacity and embed itself in UK-wide conversations.

## Decisions Made
* HEFCW will continue to support and fund growth in part-time provision.
* The sector will work to improve the accessibility of information on university income and expenditure.
* HEFCW will provide £15 million in innovation funding to help universities access UKRI funding.

## Open Questions
* The impact of Brexit on EU funding for research is still uncertain and may have far-reaching consequences for the sector.
* The sector is facing significant financial challenges, including a demographic dip and increased pension costs.
* The governing body of Trinity Saint David has taken decisions that have led to material uncertainty, but HEFCW does not see this as a governance failure.
artifacts.followups:
## Email

Subject: Follow-up on Higher Education Funding Council for Wales Meeting

Dear All,

I wanted to follow up on the meeting with the Higher Education Funding Council for Wales (HEFCW) on [Date]. The key decisions and action items discussed during the meeting were:

* HEFCW will continue to support and fund growth in part-time provision.
* The sector will work to improve the accessibility of information on university income and expenditure.
* HEFCW will provide £15 million in innovation funding to help universities access UKRI funding.

We also discussed the challenges facing the higher education sector in Wales, including demographic changes, pension costs, and Brexit uncertainty. HEFCW will continue to monitor the sector's financial sustainability and work with universities to address these challenges.

Please let me know if you have any questions or need further clarification on any of the points discussed during the meeting.

Best regards,
[Your Name]

## Slack

Reminder: Key decisions from the HEFCW meeting:

* HEFCW to continue supporting part-time provision growth
* Sector to improve transparency on university income/expenditure
* £15m innovation funding to support UKRI access

Action items: 
* HEFCW to work with universities on financial sustainability
* Sector to address demographic changes, pension costs, and Brexit uncertainty

Let me know if you have any questions or need further clarification.
artifacts.brief:
COORDINATION BRIEF

PARTICIPANTS:
- Lynne Neagle AM (Chair)
- Angela Burns AM
- Hefin David AM
- Siân Gwenllian AM
- Janet Finch-Saunders AM
- Dr David Blaney (Chief Executive, Higher Education Funding Council for Wales)
- Bethan Owen (Deputy Chief Executive, Higher Education Funding Council for Wales)

SCOPE:
The meeting discusses the financial sustainability of the higher education sector in Wales, including the impact of demographic changes, pension costs, and Brexit uncertainty. The Higher Education Funding Council for Wales (HEFCW) provides an update on their funding allocations and priorities for the 2019-20 academic year.

DECISION CUES:
- HEFCW will continue to support and fund growth in part-time provision.
- The sector will work to improve the accessibility of information on university income and expenditure.
- HEFCW will provide £15 million in innovation funding to help universities access UKRI funding.

CAUTIONS:
- The sector is facing significant financial challenges, including a demographic dip and increased pension costs.
- There is a risk that universities may struggle to maintain their attractiveness to students and maintain their research capacity.
- The impact of Brexit on EU funding for research is still uncertain and may have far-reaching consequences for the sector.
- The governing body of Trinity Saint David has taken decisions that have led to material uncertainty, but HEFCW does not see this as a governance failure.
